current_fact.fact_text: On April 13, 2023, FBI agents raided the home of airman Jack Teixeira in Massachusetts, arresting him over the leak of highly classified Pentagon documents shared in an online chat group. The case illustrated how modern secrets can slip through everyday digital spaces, blurring the line between gamer chats and global intelligence crises.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

current_fact.match_reason: A leak that forces governments and publics to reconsider what they know and trust fits an episode explicitly about rethinking assumptions and unlearning.

queue.fact_text: On April 13, 2024, a mass stabbing at the Westfield Bondi Junction shopping centre in Sydney left six victims and the attacker dead, with twelve others injured. In minutes, an ordinary weekend mall turned into a scene of chaos and collective grief for a city used to seeing Bondi as a place of sun and leisure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A sudden act of violence in a familiar public space resonates with an introspective episode on bodily vulnerability, recovery, and the slow work of healing after trauma.

queue.fact_text: On April 13, 2022–23’s Pentagon document leaks case highlighted how classified intelligence can now move at the speed of screenshots and memes through online communities. What once would have required spies and couriers can now be triggered by a teenager behind a keyboard.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The same case, seen through the lens of how digital natives learn, mishandle, and understand information, connects with an episode about the brain, mistakes, and slow learning in a hyper-fast world.

queue.fact_text: On April 13, 1943, as reports of the Katyn graves emerged, the Soviet Union broke diplomatic relations with the Polish government-in-exile, insisting Nazi Germany was to blame. For decades, history itself became a battleground, with archives and narratives weaponised long after the shooting stopped.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: Competing wartime narratives and state 'masks' over historical truth echo an episode about alter-egos, constructed identities, and the stories we choose to present.

queue.fact_text: On April 13, 1948’s Hadassah medical convoy massacre, the targeting of doctors and nurses on a clearly marked mission shocked observers worldwide. It remains a stark reminder that in polarized conflicts, even the symbols of care and neutrality can be stripped of their protection.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: In contrast to violence against healers, this fluid, immersive set offers listeners a gentle space to go 'below the surface' of headlines into feeling and empathy.

queue.fact_text: On April 13, 1964, when Sidney Poitier accepted his Oscar, he not only earned a statuette but also redefined the possibilities for Black actors to take complex, dignified roles. That night rippled forward, influencing generations of artists who would insist on telling their stories their own way.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Poitier’s historic 'first' and its long afterglow mirror Sedna FM’s own first episode: a raw beginning whose impact shapes everything that comes next.

published.fact_text: In the early hours of April 13, 1970, the Apollo 13 mission turned from a Moon landing into a desperate survival story when an oxygen tank in the service module exploded. The crew’s calm collaboration with ground control under extreme pressure gave birth to the phrase “Houston, we’ve had a problem,” and turned near-disaster into a legendary lesson in improvisation and resilience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spaceflight crisis that required a mental 'space shift' fits an episode about transforming emptiness and pressure into creative problem‑solving energy.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1960, the United States launched Transit 1-B, the world’s first operational satellite navigation system. Long before GPS in smartphones, sailors and submarines were already quietly using space-borne radio signals to find their way across the oceans.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A pioneering navigation satellite mirrors a musical journey that glides from one sonic landscape to another, using sound like a compass through global nighttime skies.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1742, George Frideric Handel’s oratorio *Messiah* premiered in Dublin, Ireland, to such demand that women were asked not to wear hooped skirts so more people could fit into the hall. The work, written in just 24 days, would go on to become one of the most frequently performed choral pieces in history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A timeless sacred work that keeps returning every season pairs with an episode about music that feels perennial, like a liturgical 'endless summer' of sound.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1964, Sidney Poitier became the first African-American man to win the Academy Award for Best Actor, honored for his role in *Lilies of the Field*. His win cracked open doors in Hollywood, challenging decades of racist typecasting and redefining who could be seen as a leading man.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: Poitier’s barrier‑breaking Oscar win aligns with an episode celebrating iconic voices who reshape representation and dignity through performance.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1699, Guru Gobind Singh formalised the Khalsa, transforming Sikhism into a community of 'Warrior-Saints' bound by shared initiation, code, and identity. The ceremony at Anandpur Sahib turned faith into a disciplined collective, blending spirituality with the courage to resist oppression.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: The Khalsa’s blend of spirituality, identity, and resistance resonates with Tuareg music shaped by faith, land, and struggle for autonomy.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1612, legendary swordsman Miyamoto Musashi defeated Sasaki Kojirō in a duel on Ganryū-jima, arriving late and wielding a wooden sword he had carved from an oar. The fight lasted only moments, but it became one of Japan’s most mythologized stories of strategy, psychology, and unconventional technique.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A legendary duel shaped by training and mental edge fits an episode grounded in combat sports, discipline, and the art of movement.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1204, Crusader forces brutally sacked Constantinople during the Fourth Crusade, looting one of the richest cities on Earth and shattering the Byzantine Empire. Countless manuscripts, artworks, and relics were destroyed or scattered across Europe, permanently altering the cultural map of the Mediterranean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A cataclysmic event at the crossroads of the Mediterranean pairs with an episode sailing gently through the region’s modern musical echoes, from Greek to Turkish to Italian coasts.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1777, during the American Revolutionary War, Continental forces were surprised and defeated at the Battle of Bound Brook in New Jersey. Though a tactical setback, the skirmish sharpened American awareness of British movements and the difficulty of defending a scattered young army.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A revolutionary defeat in a broader struggle for independence connects with an episode steeped in politically charged music about resistance and power.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1945, Soviet and Bulgarian troops captured Vienna after weeks of heavy fighting, ending Nazi control of the city. Beneath the rubble and ruined concert halls, one of Europe’s great music capitals began the long process of rebuilding its cultural life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The violent 'shock' of a city being seized and then reborn mirrors an episode built around jolting electric guitars and explosive energy.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1943, the discovery of mass graves in the Katyn Forest revealed that thousands of Polish officers had been executed and buried in secrecy. For decades, the Soviet Union denied responsibility, turning a crime scene into a long-running geopolitical lie before finally admitting the truth in 1990.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A painful chapter in Polish history fits with an episode literally traveling through Poland, giving sonic space to the country’s landscapes and memories.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1948, a medical convoy headed to Hadassah Hospital in Jerusalem was ambushed in Sheikh Jarrah, and 79 people were killed, including doctors, nurses, and a British soldier. The attack underscored how even vehicles marked for healing can become targets in times of spiraling conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A harrowing episode where healers are attacked is balanced with a contemplative, cinematic set that offers room for mourning and quiet reflection.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1953, CIA director Allen Dulles authorised Project MKUltra, a covert program that experimented with mind-altering drugs, hypnosis, and sensory deprivation, often without subjects’ consent. The project’s existence wasn’t fully exposed until the 1970s, sparking public outrage over ethics, secrecy, and the manipulation of human consciousness.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A secret program probing and manipulating minds sits alongside an episode unpacking our modern, voluntary entanglement with technology and mental overload.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1976, the Lapua Cartridge Factory in Finland exploded, killing 40 workers in what remains the country’s deadliest modern industrial accident. The blast was so powerful it shattered windows kilometres away and led to major reforms in workplace safety and explosives handling.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, devastating explosion as an industrial 'shock' links thematically to an episode built around high-voltage intensity and the raw power of controlled energy.

published.fact_text: Also on April 13, 1976, the United States reintroduced the two-dollar bill as part of its Bicentennial celebrations, featuring a new reverse design of the signing of the Declaration of Independence. Despite official status, the note never became common in circulation, feeding urban legends and making it a quirky favourite among collectors.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A quirky, underused banknote reintroduced as a 'fresh start' fits an upbeat, on‑the‑move mix about beginnings, circulation, and things in motion.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1972, the Universal Postal Union voted to recognise the People’s Republic of China as the sole representative of China, effectively expelling Taiwan from the global postal system’s formal structures. Even the humble act of sending letters suddenly reflected the world’s shifting diplomatic alignments.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A treaty organisation coordinating the world’s mail aligns with an episode about international movement and quietly shifting perspectives across borders.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1975, a bus carrying Palestinian fighters and civilians was ambushed in Beirut, killing 26 and igniting the Lebanese Civil War. What began as a single attack spiraled into fifteen years of complex, multi-sided conflict that reshaped Lebanon’s society and diaspora.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: A massacre that helped tear Lebanon apart connects poignantly with an episode featuring 'Lebanese Blonde' and a genre born from urban tension, memory, and cinematic melancholy.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1996, an Israeli helicopter fired rockets at an ambulance in the Lebanese village of Mansouri, killing two women and four children and injuring many others. The strike, which Israel said targeted militants, became a stark symbol of how civilians are caught in the crossfire of asymmetrical warfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A tragic episode of civilian loss calls for a slow, reflective musical space, offering listeners a quiet planetary pause to sit with the weight of such events.

published.fact_text: On April 13, 1997, at just 21 years old, Tiger Woods won the Masters Tournament, becoming its youngest champion and the first African-American golfer to claim the green jacket. His 12-stroke victory shattered records and barriers, fundamentally changing who many people imagined could dominate golf’s most traditional stage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A story of breakthrough, perseverance, and a new era in sport pairs with an episode about moving through storms toward hopeful change and sunny fairways ahead.

The case illustrated how modern secrets can slip through everyday digital spaces, blurring the line between gamer chats and global intelligence crises.
